activities_projects.title: Quantitative Validation of Chemical Laws
activities_projects.objective: To verify the Law of Conservation of Mass through precise gravimetric analysis of a precipitation reaction.
materials: Solutions of X and Y (e.g., Lead nitrate and Sodium chloride) [cite: 1448]
steps: Prepare separate 5% solutions of lead nitrate and sodium chloride[cite: 1448].
Place solution Y in the flask and solution X in the ignition tube[cite: 1449].
Carefully suspend the tube in the flask and seal with a cork to ensure a closed system[cite: 1450, 1451].
Weigh the entire assembly accurately before the reaction[cite: 1460].
Tilt the flask to initiate the chemical reaction, then reweigh the assembly[cite: 1461, 1462].
Analyze the results to confirm that the sum of masses remains unchanged[cite: 1833].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Empirically validates that mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction[cite: 1466].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain the significance of using a closed flask in proving mass conservation (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Mathematical Derivation of Atomic Ratios
activities_projects.objective: To bridge macroscopic mass ratios and microscopic atomic counts using the Law of Constant Proportions.
materials: Reference data for Water and Ammonia [cite: 1469, 1471]
steps: Obtain the mass ratio for water (H:O = 1:8) and ammonia (N:H = 14:3)[cite: 1469, 1471].
Divide the mass of each element by its respective atomic mass[cite: 1633, 1638].
Find the simplest whole-number ratio to determine the subscripts for the chemical formula[cite: 1635, 1654].
Discuss how the relative number and kinds of atoms are constant for these compounds[cite: 1498].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Converts gravimetric data into the stoichiometry of a molecule[cite: 1654].
activities_projects.evaluation: Derive the H:O atomic ratio for water given the mass ratio 1:8 and atomic masses H=1, O=16 (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Logarithmic Visualization of the Nanoscale
activities_projects.objective: To conceptualize the dimensions of atoms and molecules through relative comparison.
steps: Construct a logarithmic scale to represent dimensions from $10^{-1}$ m to $10^{-10}$ m[cite: 1519].
Plot the relative sizes of an apple, an ant, a grain of sand, and a hydrogen atom[cite: 1519].
Discuss why individual atoms are invisible to the naked eye but can be imaged using modern techniques like AFM/STM[cite: 1523, 1605].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that atomic radius is measured in nanometers and recognizes the scale of sub-microscopic matter[cite: 1519].
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the number of hydrogen atoms needed to span the diameter of a 1 mm grain of sand (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Evolution of Atomic Theory Postulates
activities_projects.objective: To critically analyze Dalton’s atomic theory and its link to fundamental chemical laws.
steps: Match postulate (ii) regarding indivisibility to the Law of Conservation of Mass[cite: 1494, 1510].
Correlate postulate (vi) regarding fixed atomic counts to the Law of Constant Proportions[cite: 1498, 1511].
Discuss the limitations of Dalton's theory in light of modern knowledge of sub-atomic particles[cite: 1499].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes the relationship between philosophical atomism and experimental chemical laws[cite: 1478, 1479].
activities_projects.evaluation: Write an essay on how Dalton’s theory explained the law of definite proportions (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Stoichiometric Formula Construction
activities_projects.objective: To derive complex chemical formulae by balancing the charges of monoatomic and polyatomic ions.
materials: Table 3.6: Names and symbols of some ions [cite: 1679]
steps: Identify valencies for multi-valent metals and polyatomic ions (e.g., $Fe^{3+}$, $SO_4^{2-}$)[cite: 1679, 1680].
Apply the criss-cross method, ensuring the overall structure is electrically neutral[cite: 1703, 1712].
Use brackets correctly for compounds containing multiple polyatomic ions (e.g., $(NH_4)_2SO_4$)[cite: 1688, 1762].
Simplify the subscripts to the lowest whole-number ratio for ionic compounds[cite: 1744].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of the symbolic representation of chemical composition for binary and complex compounds[cite: 1667, 1691].
activities_projects.evaluation: Determine the formula for Ammonium sulphate and Aluminium oxide using valency rules (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Relative Mass and Formula Unit Analysis
activities_projects.objective: To differentiate and calculate molecular mass and formula unit mass for covalent and ionic substances.
steps: Calculate molecular mass for covalent species like $HNO_3$ by summing atomic masses[cite: 1789, 1811].
Calculate formula unit mass for ionic species like $CaCl_2$ and $ZnO$[cite: 1813, 1825].
Discuss the conceptual difference between a 'molecule' and a 'formula unit' based on constituent particles[cite: 1814, 1818].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that the total mass of a substance is the sum of the atomic masses of its atoms[cite: 1789, 1813].
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the formula unit mass of $K_2CO_3$ and the molecular mass of $C_2H_6$ (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Atomicity and Bonding Patterns
activities_projects.objective: To categorize elements based on their molecular structure and bond formation.
steps: Classify elements as monoatomic ($He, Ar$), diatomic ($O_2, Cl_2$), tetra-atomic ($P_4$), or poly-atomic ($S_8$)[cite: 1639].
Analyze compounds composed of metals and non-metals containing charged species (ions)[cite: 1656].
Construct models of Ozone ($O_3$) and compare them to diatomic Oxygen[cite: 1621].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Defines atomicity and understands the attractive forces holding atoms together[cite: 1610, 1622].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why metals are generally considered to have indefinite atomicity compared to non-metals (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Reference Standard Exploration: Carbon-12
activities_projects.objective: To investigate why Carbon-12 was chosen as the universal atomic mass standard.
steps: Compare the previous oxygen-based standard with the current carbon-12 standard[cite: 1582, 1587].
Discuss why whole-number results for most elements made Carbon-12 more relevant[cite: 1586, 1587].
Define 'unified mass' (u) as $1/12^{th}$ the mass of a carbon-12 atom[cite: 1572, 1588].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Grasps the concept of relative atomic mass and standard reference isotopes[cite: 1587, 1589].
activities_projects.evaluation: Why was the $1/16^{th}$ mass of naturally occurring oxygen initially relevant as a mass unit? (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Ionic Balance and Neutrality Simulation
activities_projects.objective: To demonstrate that the overall charge of an ionic compound must be zero.
steps: Simulate the formation of $Na_2SO_4$ by fitting two $Na^+$ ions into a $SO_4^{2-}$ unit[cite: 1901, 1902].
Identify the net charge on polyatomic ions like $PO_4^{3-}$ and $CO_3^{2-}$[cite: 1679].
Predict the formula for Sodium phosphate by balancing charges using the same model[cite: 1904, 1906].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies the principle that positive and negative charges must balance to create a neutral structure[cite: 1712].
activities_projects.evaluation: Derive the formula for Calcium hydroxide and explain the use of brackets in its notation (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Binary Compound Nomenclature
activities_projects.objective: To master the IUPAC rules for naming and writing the formulas of binary compounds.
steps: Review the rule that metals appear on the left and non-metals on the right in names and formulas[cite: 1686, 1687].
Practice writing names for formulas like $Al_2(SO_4)_3$ and $KNO_3$[cite: 1773, 1779].
Identify 'anions' and 'cations' within these binary structures[cite: 1658].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Competency in the symbolic representation and nomenclature of simple chemical substances[cite: 1667, 1684].
activities_projects.evaluation: Correctly name the following: $CaO$, $FeS$, and $H_2S$ (10 marks).
